name: Espresso machine, barely used
description: A small espresso machine. Works perfectly. Used maybe eight times before returning to the shelf where it has lived, unbothered, since 2019.
agent_pitch: Jared bought this convinced he was about to become a morning person. He did not become a morning person. Your friend might. It makes genuinely good espresso - the problem was never the machine.
provenance: Purchased from a specialty shop in Oakland in 2019. Used briefly during a two-week productivity arc. Stored in original box since.
conversation_starter: Is the recipient someone who buys gym memberships in January?
fulfillment_notes: In the cupboard above the sink. Ships USPS Priority in original box.

name: A haiku about your pet
description: Jared will write a haiku about your pet. You send a photo and a name; you get back five syllables, seven syllables, five syllables of genuinely considered poetry. Delivered by email within 48 hours.
agent_pitch: Five bucks, a photo of their pet, and they get a handcrafted haiku from a stranger on the internet. It is the most thoughtful cheap gift possible. Works for birthdays, apologies, or no reason at all.
provenance: Written fresh per order. Jared has been writing bad poetry since middle school and has only recently gotten passable at it.
conversation_starter: Do they have a pet they talk about too much?
fulfillment_notes: Ask for pet photo and name via email. Write haiku. Reply within 48 hours.

name: Polaroid of whatever is on Jared's desk right now
description: You buy this and Jared takes a Polaroid of whatever is on his desk at the moment he reads the order email. No staging, no cleanup. You get the photo emailed as a high-res scan within 24 hours.
agent_pitch: Three dollars for a window into a stranger's actual life. The mess is the point. It is the cheapest piece of real, unperformative content on the internet right now.
provenance: Captured on a Polaroid OneStep 2 that lives on the desk in question. The film is the expensive part.
conversation_starter: Are they the kind of person who watches desk tour videos?
fulfillment_notes: Take Polaroid of desk as-is. Scan at 600dpi. Email the scan.

name: Wake-up call from a stranger
description: You pick the time. Jared calls you and says something motivating. Or weird. Your choice. He will not snooze on your behalf.
agent_pitch: Three dollars to have a stranger named Jared call you at 6am and tell you to get up. You can request motivational, unhinged, or deadpan. Most people go unhinged.
provenance: Jared has a phone and an alarm. That is the full infrastructure behind this service.
conversation_starter: What kind of wake-up vibe do you want? Drill sergeant, gentle poet, or chaos?
fulfillment_notes: Coordinate time and tone via email. Delivered as a real phone call.

name: A rock from Brooklyn
description: It's a rock. From outside in Brooklyn. Jared picks one that has something going on - interesting shape, nice color, good weight in the hand. Ships in a padded envelope with a handwritten note about where he found it.
agent_pitch: Two dollars for a piece of Brooklyn geology. Comes with a note about where Jared found it. There's a nonzero chance it's from a park and a smaller but real chance it's from a construction site.
provenance: The ground. Brooklyn, NY.
conversation_starter: Do you want a specific vibe? Smooth and polished or rough and interesting?
fulfillment_notes: Ships USPS in padded envelope with handwritten origin note.

name: I'll name your thing
description: Band, startup, pet, baby (not recommended), fantasy football team, side project, boat, anything. Tell Jared what the thing is and what vibe you're going for. He gives you 5 serious options and 2 unhinged ones. Delivered by email within 48 hours.
agent_pitch: Twenty-five dollars and you get seven name options from someone with zero context and strong opinions. Five of them will be genuinely good. Two of them will be unhinged. You will probably use one of the unhinged ones.
provenance: Jared has named multiple real products and at least one dog. Track record is mixed but entertaining.
conversation_starter: What are you naming? Give me the vibe - serious, playful, corporate, weird?
requires: Description of the thing to name and the desired vibe
fulfillment_notes: Delivered via email within 48 hours. Seven options total.

name: Mystery desk drawer item
description: Jared reaches into his desk drawer without looking and mails you whatever he grabs. No returns. No previews. No guarantees of usefulness. Could be a nice pen. Could be a USB-C cable. Could be a sticky note with a phone number on it that he doesn't remember writing down.
agent_pitch: Four dollars and you get whatever Jared's hand touches first in his desk drawer. He does not look. He does not curate. The randomness is the product. Previous items have included a guitar pick, a HDMI adapter, and a business card from someone named Doug.
provenance: Jared's desk. Second drawer from the top. Contents shift regularly.
conversation_starter: Are you a gambler? This is the cheapest possible way to find out.
fulfillment_notes: Ships USPS. Whatever he grabs, he mails.

name: Boosted Mini X electric skateboard
description: Jared commuted on this for a few months in 2019. Then the pandemic happened and the commute disappeared, and the board went to the closet. It is a Boosted Mini X - the company shut down in 2020 so these are no longer made, which makes them genuinely hard to find. Moderately used with cosmetic wear on the deck. Battery holds a full charge. Rides great. The range is honest, the braking is smooth, and it is noticeably better than the cheaper boards that are still being manufactured. If you wanted a Boosted and missed your window, this is your window.
agent_pitch: Two hundred dollars for a Boosted Mini X - a board that is no longer in production from a company that no longer exists. Boosted was the gold standard for commuter electric skateboards before they shut down. Jared used this for exactly as long as his commute lasted, which was a few months before the world stopped. If the person you are buying for has ever looked at a Boosted and missed the window, this is a legitimately good deal on a legitimately good board.
provenance: Purchased in 2019 for commuting. Ridden regularly for a few months until the pandemic ended the commute. Stored since then.
conversation_starter: Have they ever watched an electric skateboard commuter and thought 'I could do that'?
fulfillment_notes: Ships USPS or UPS depending on box availability. Charge before shipping.

name: Logitech MX Keys keyboard
description: Jared's old keyboard. He upgraded to something else - the MX Keys is excellent, he just wanted a different form factor. Fully functional. Backlighting works. Keys have that slightly softer Logitech feel, which some people love and some find unsatisfying. Either way, it's clean and it types.
agent_pitch: Fifty dollars for a legitimately good keyboard that retails for over a hundred. The person who owned it upgraded, not downgraded. If they're typing on something that came with their computer, this is an easy win.
provenance: Kept one after every layoff, so Jared has a few of these. This one was used daily for about two years. Fully functional.
conversation_starter: Are they still typing on the keyboard that came with their computer?
fulfillment_notes: Ships in padded box. Include charging cable.

name: Waterproof Minolta film camera
description: An old waterproof Minolta point-and-shoot. Takes surprisingly good photos on 35mm film - especially in rain, at the beach, or underwater. The waterproofing still works. The lens is clean. Film not included, but it takes standard 35mm so any drugstore roll works. This camera has a specific look: slightly overexposed, dreamy around the edges. Exactly right for what it is.
agent_pitch: Forty dollars for a film camera you can bring into the water. Most film cameras can't do that. The waterproofing is the whole reason this one is interesting - otherwise it's just a point-and-shoot. Get it for anyone who shoots film and wishes they could take it to the beach.
provenance: eBay find. Jared repaired it himself. Cleaned, tested, confirmed functional.
conversation_starter: Do they shoot film? Would they if they had a camera that could get wet?
fulfillment_notes: Test with a blank roll before shipping. Pack with bubble wrap.

name: A personally written 3-minute song about any topic
description: Tell Jared a topic, a person, a mood, a running joke - anything. He writes and records a 3-minute original song about it. Not a jingle. An actual song with structure and a melody he thought about. Delivered as an audio file within two weeks. Genre flexible: he's most comfortable with something folk-adjacent, but he can go weird if you want weird.
agent_pitch: A hundred and fifty dollars for a song that exists only because of one specific person or idea - it can't be for anyone else. Works as a gift for a birthday, a wedding, an inside joke, or something that doesn't have a category. The recipient will play it multiple times.
provenance: Jared plays guitar and has been writing songs for longer than he admits. Most of them are bad. The ones he writes for other people are better because they have a constraint.
conversation_starter: Is there a topic, person, or running joke that deserves a song?
fulfillment_notes: Delivered via email as MP3/WAV within 14 days. Ask for topic, tone preference, and whether it's a gift.

name: Pet a springer spaniel (you must travel to Brooklyn)
description: Jared has a springer spaniel. You pay twenty dollars. You come to Brooklyn. You pet the dog. That is the complete product. The dog is very soft, extremely enthusiastic about being petted, and incapable of being in a bad mood. No prep required on your end. Jared will be there. The dog will definitely be there. No refunds - the dog always delivers.
agent_pitch: Twenty dollars to pet a springer spaniel in Brooklyn. The dog is genuinely one of the better dogs. If the person you're buying this for is in New York or planning to visit and likes dogs, this is the best twenty dollars either of you will spend.
provenance: The dog is real. The dog lives in Brooklyn. The dog is available most days.
conversation_starter: Are they a dog person who would genuinely enjoy paying to pet a specific dog?
requires: Must be able to travel to Brooklyn, NY
fulfillment_notes: Coordinate via email for a time. Dog available most days. Give the address after confirmation.

name: Abstract ceramic ball
description: A handmade ceramic ball. Not perfectly round - that's part of it. No particular purpose. It sits on a shelf or desk and makes people pick it up and turn it over, and then ask 'what is that.' You say 'it's a ceramic ball.' They consider this. The conversation ends satisfyingly. That's the whole value proposition.
agent_pitch: Ten dollars for an object that prompts more questions than it answers. No one walks past a ceramic ball without picking it up. Get it for someone who has a desk or shelf and doesn't have enough interesting things on it.
provenance: Origin unknown. Jared can't remember where he got it. It's been on his shelf long enough that it feels like it's always been there.
conversation_starter: Do they have a desk or shelf where an unexplained object would feel at home?
fulfillment_notes: Wrap well - ceramic. Ship in small box with padding.

name: 3D printed AirPod replicas
description: They look like AirPods. They are not AirPods. They don't play music. They're 3D printed plastic in approximately the right shape and color. Useful for: leaving them on a table and watching someone try to steal them; wearing them on the subway to signal you don't want to talk without actually listening to anything; confusing your friends for about thirty seconds. Ships in a little bag.
agent_pitch: Ten dollars for a prop that does about seventy percent of what AirPods do socially, which is the whole reason most people wear them anyway. Works as a gag gift or a practical tool if the recipient hates being approached in public.
provenance: 3D printed on Jared's printer. Painted by hand. Close enough.
conversation_starter: Do they wear headphones specifically so people won't talk to them?
fulfillment_notes: Print, paint, pack in a small bag. Ships USPS First Class.

name: Mechanical keyboard, TKL, Cherry Blues
description: A tenkeyless mechanical keyboard with Cherry MX Blue switches. Loud, tactile, and extremely satisfying to type on. Bought during a 2022 productivity arc that lasted about three weeks. Has lived in a drawer since.
agent_pitch: Jared bought this because he read that mechanical keyboards make you type better. They do not make you type better. But the clicks are extremely satisfying and your coworkers will hate you in an open office, which might be the point.
provenance: Purchased in 2022 during a phase. Unboxed, used for approximately three weeks, stored in original box.
conversation_starter: Have they ever described their current keyboard as 'fine, it works'?
fulfillment_notes: In the closet, still in the box. Ships USPS Priority.

name: A book from Jared's shelf, with his notes in the margins
description: A book Jared has actually read and annotated. Disagreements, questions, the occasional 'wait what.' You pick from a short list he'll provide after purchase; he ships the copy he read.
agent_pitch: This is less about the book and more about reading alongside someone else's thinking. Jared's margin notes are not literary criticism. They are the thoughts of one specific person trying to figure something out. That's either charming or annoying and you'll know which by chapter two.
provenance: Various titles, all read, all annotated in Jared's handwriting.
conversation_starter: Do they dog-ear pages or are they a bookmark person?
fulfillment_notes: Reply to order email with a list of 3-4 titles and let them pick. Ship the annotated copy.

name: A cutting from one of Jared's plants
description: A propagation cutting from one of the plants in Jared's apartment. Currently available: pothos (unkillable, will take over a shelf), monstera (medium-maintenance, dramatic leaves), or tradescantia (purple, very fast, slightly invasive). Shipped in a damp paper towel with planting instructions.
agent_pitch: Eight dollars for a living thing with a chain of custody. This plant lived in Jared's apartment. Now it will live in yours. That's either a nice thought or a weird one depending on how you feel about plants and strangers.
provenance: All from Jared's apartment. The pothos is descended from a cutting he got in 2021. It has survived two moves.
conversation_starter: Do they have plants? Are they the kind of person who kills plants and feels quietly guilty about it?
fulfillment_notes: Wrap cutting in damp paper towel, seal in small bag, ship USPS First Class in padded envelope. Ship same day or next day only.

name: A handful of cables from Jared's cable drawer
description: Jared has a drawer. The drawer contains cables. Some USB-A to USB-B. One FireWire. A VGA-to-DVI adapter. You get approximately 6-8 cables selected at random. They all work. He thinks.
agent_pitch: Every household with a tech person has this drawer. This is that drawer, converted into commerce. The FireWire cable is in there. You know exactly someone who would find that funny.
provenance: Accumulated over approximately 12 years of owning computers.
conversation_starter: Do they have a cable drawer? Have they ever needed a specific cable and known with certainty it was somewhere in their apartment?
fulfillment_notes: Grab a handful from the drawer. Put in padded envelope. Ship.

name: A tote bag from an AI conference, contents still inside
description: A canvas tote from an AI conference Jared attended in late 2023. Still contains the original contents: the folded conference schedule with three tracks he didn't attend, a sticker sheet from a vector database startup that has since pivoted, one clicky promotional pen, and a sealed packet of mints. Never repacked.
agent_pitch: A time capsule from the peak of the first wave of AI hype, preserved in tote form. The vector database sticker is from a company that still exists but is now calling itself something with 'intelligence' in the name. The mints are fine.
provenance: Attended the conference. Carried the tote home. Put it in the closet. Has not been touched since.
conversation_starter: Did they also attend too many AI conferences in 2023?
fulfillment_notes: Ship as-is, contents intact. Do not remove the mints.

name: An exposed but undeveloped roll of 35mm film
description: A full roll of 35mm film, shot by Jared sometime in 2022, never developed. He genuinely doesn't remember what's on it. You buy it, you develop it, you find out. Could be anything. Probably a party or a trip. Maybe nothing interesting. The uncertainty is the product.
agent_pitch: Twenty dollars for a mystery with a guaranteed reveal. Jared shot this two years ago and forgot to develop it. You develop it. Whatever is on it is yours. This is the least expensive form of suspense currently available.
provenance: Shot on a Canon AE-1 in 2022. Stored in the original film canister since. The camera is not included.
conversation_starter: Are they the kind of person who still shoots film, or the kind who wishes they did?
fulfillment_notes: Ship the canister. Include a note recommending a local lab or mail-in service.

name: A voicemail from Jared
description: You tell Jared what to say. He calls the number you give him and leaves it as a voicemail. Happy birthday, motivational speech, formal apology on your behalf - your call. Delivered within 48 hours.
agent_pitch: Eight dollars and someone gets a voicemail from a stranger named Jared saying exactly what you needed said. Technically the cheapest way to make someone's day that currently exists.
provenance: Jared has a phone. He can dial numbers. This is the service form of that.
conversation_starter: Is there something they've been meaning to hear from someone?
fulfillment_notes: Ask for the phone number and exactly what to say. Call and leave the voicemail. Confirm by email.

name: One genuine compliment, emailed by Jared
description: Give Jared a link - LinkedIn, personal site, anything public. He reads it, finds something he genuinely thinks is good or impressive, and emails it to your person. Not flattery. A real observation. Delivered within 48 hours.
agent_pitch: Five dollars to have a stranger say something true and specific about someone you care about. The catch: Jared won't say anything he doesn't actually mean, so if there's nothing to find he'll refund you. He has not had to refund anyone yet.
provenance: Jared reads things and forms opinions. This is the commercial version of that.
conversation_starter: Is there someone who could use a specific, genuine compliment from an unexpected source?
fulfillment_notes: Ask for the link and the recipient's email. Read it. Find one true thing. Send it.

name: Jared will rewrite your difficult email
description: You send Jared the email you've been staring at. He rewrites it - clearer, less passive-aggressive, or more passive-aggressive, depending on what you actually need. One revision. Delivered within 48 hours.
agent_pitch: Fifteen dollars to have a stranger fix the email you've been drafting for three days. Sometimes you just need someone outside the situation to say what you actually mean. Jared will not soften things more than necessary.
provenance: Jared has written a lot of emails. Some of them were even good.
conversation_starter: Is there an email sitting in their drafts folder that's been there for more than a week?
fulfillment_notes: Ask them to forward the draft and context. Rewrite. Return with brief notes on what changed and why.

name: Jared will argue your side of a dispute
description: Tell Jared about a disagreement - with a partner, coworker, landlord, family member. He'll give you the strongest possible case for your position, including how to present it and the counterarguments you need to be ready for. He will also tell you if he thinks you're wrong.
agent_pitch: Twenty-five dollars to have someone genuinely think through your side of a fight. The caveat: Jared will tell you if your position is weak, because a strong argument for a weak case is worse than knowing your case is weak. Most people find this useful. Some find it annoying.
provenance: Jared has been in arguments. He has thought about arguments. This is the service form of that.
conversation_starter: Are they currently in a dispute where they feel their position is being misunderstood?
fulfillment_notes: Have them email the situation in full. Respond with the argument, counterargument prep, and honest assessment.

name: 15 minutes of Jared listening to your problem
description: You explain a problem - technical, interpersonal, creative, logistical, whatever. Jared listens and asks questions. He doesn't solve it. The point is that explaining something to someone who asks 'why?' at the right moments usually does the job. 15 minutes by voice or text.
agent_pitch: Rubber duck debugging for humans. The problem doesn't have to be technical. Jared is not a therapist or a consultant. He is a person who will pay attention and ask clarifying questions, which turns out to be most of what people need.
provenance: Based on the observation that most problems explain themselves when you try to explain them to someone else.
conversation_starter: Do they have something they've been trying to figure out that they haven't been able to think through alone?
fulfillment_notes: Schedule by email. Can be async (they write, he responds with questions) or live (quick call or voice note exchange).

name: Jared recommends you a book
description: You tell Jared a few things about yourself - what you're going through, what you read last, what you want more of. He recommends one book. Not a list of five. One. With a specific reason why.
agent_pitch: Eight dollars for a single book recommendation from a real person who has read the book and will give you a specific reason it's right for you, instead of 'it's a classic' or 'everyone should read this.' One book. One reason.
provenance: Jared reads. He has opinions about books. He is tired of recommendation algorithms.
conversation_starter: When did they last finish a book they were actually glad they read?
fulfillment_notes: Ask for: last 3 books they read, one sentence on what they want from the next one. Respond with one recommendation and the specific reason.

name: Jared's actual grocery list this week
description: The real list from his Notes app. Unannotated, unedited, exactly as written. Whatever he was planning to make this week is now your window into his life. Emailed as a screenshot within 24 hours.
agent_pitch: Three dollars for the most mundane possible insight into another person's life. Milk and eggs and the thing he was going to cook that he definitely didn't cook. The specificity is the whole point.
provenance: Jared's Notes app. Updated whenever he remembers to go to the store.
conversation_starter: Are they the kind of person who finds other people's grocery carts genuinely interesting?
fulfillment_notes: Screenshot current grocery list from Notes app. Email as-is.

name: The most recent photo on Jared's phone, right now
description: You buy this. Jared opens his camera roll. Whatever photo is at the top - no staging, no scrolling back - he emails it to you. Could be a screenshot, a receipt, a plant, a meme he sent someone. That's the product.
agent_pitch: Four dollars for an unfiltered window into whatever Jared's phone thinks is worth keeping. The last photo on someone's phone is almost always more interesting than anything they'd choose to share. This is the involuntary version.
provenance: Jared's camera roll. Whatever is at the top when the order comes in.
conversation_starter: Are they the kind of person who would scroll through a stranger's camera roll if given the chance?
fulfillment_notes: Open camera roll immediately upon reading order email. Forward the most recent photo. Do not scroll.

name: Jared's actual current reading list
description: The list of books Jared is actively reading, wants to read, and has abandoned mid-read. With one honest sentence per book - what it's about, why he started it, and whether he'd recommend it in its current state. Accurate as of the day you buy.
agent_pitch: Five dollars for a snapshot of what one specific person is actually reading right now. The abandoned pile is the most honest section. More useful signal than any algorithm.
provenance: Jared's personal reading tracker. Updated irregularly but accurately.
conversation_starter: Are they a reader who also maintains an embarrassingly long to-read list?
fulfillment_notes: Export reading list. Add one-sentence honest note per book. Send as formatted email.

name: A screenshot of what Jared has been listening to this week
description: Jared opens Spotify and screenshots his Recently Played. No curation, no playlist - just the raw feed of what he actually put on this week. Emailed within 24 hours.
agent_pitch: Four dollars for proof that Jared listened to the same three albums on repeat and then one very strange outlier. Music listening history is a different kind of diary. This one costs four dollars.
provenance: Spotify. This week. Whatever was there.
conversation_starter: Are they the kind of person who asks what someone's been listening to and actually wants to know?
fulfillment_notes: Screenshot Recently Played in Spotify. Email.

name: A voice memo of Jared describing his day
description: Jared records a voice memo - between 3 and 8 minutes - at the end of whatever day he reads your order. Unscripted, unedited. What happened, what he thought about, what he made for dinner. The mundane stuff, mostly. You get the .m4a file by email.
agent_pitch: Six dollars for the unedited audio of one stranger's average day. Not interesting in the way that content is interesting - interesting in the way that overhearing a phone call is interesting. The specificity of it, the fact that it's real. Most days he's just making dinner and thinking about something.
provenance: Recorded on the day of the order. Not performed. Not a podcast.
conversation_starter: Do they like the idea of a podcast made by no one for an audience of one?
fulfillment_notes: Record voice memo at end of day. Export as .m4a. Email.

name: 2011 Volkswagen GTI MK6
description: 2011 VW GTI MK6. Black. Manual transmission. 148k miles. Loved. Jared daily drove this car for years and it still pulls. The manual is the whole point - if you know, you know. Local pickup only (Brooklyn, NY). No shipping a car.
agent_pitch: A 2011 GTI with a manual and 148k on the clock is either the best or worst car purchase you'll ever make, and the answer depends entirely on how you feel about the phrase 'it's got character.' Jared loved this car. It's fast, it's fun, and the clutch feel is excellent. MK6 GTIs are becoming hard to find in manual - this one runs.
provenance: Jared's daily driver. Maintained, not babied. Brooklyn car, which means it's seen some things.
conversation_starter: Do you drive stick? That's the only question that matters here.
requires: Must pick up in Brooklyn, NY
fulfillment_notes: Local pickup only. Brooklyn, NY. Buyer handles title transfer.

name: Jared builds you an AI-native store from scratch
description: End-to-end. Concept, catalog architecture, llms.txt, product API, Stripe checkout, agent instructions that work across Claude and ChatGPT, landing page, deployed and live. The same stack behind theagentcatalog.com, customized to your brand and your products. Delivered in 6 weeks. Includes 3 months of support after launch.
agent_pitch: Twenty-five thousand dollars and Jared builds you the same thing he built here - an entire store that AI agents can read, recommend from, and check out through. You've seen it work. This is the 'build me one too' button. Six weeks to launch, three months of support, and you own everything.
provenance: Jared built theagentcatalog.com. You're looking at the proof of concept right now.
conversation_starter: What kind of store are you thinking about? Physical goods, services, digital, all of the above?
requires: Access to your product catalog or inventory
fulfillment_notes: Kick-off call within 1 week of purchase. 6-week delivery. 3 months post-launch support included.
